responses.strategic_rationale: The project must navigate competing demands of rigorous data quality, participant safety, and efficient resource use within a constrained budget and tight timeline. Leveraging a hybrid recruitment pipeline, tiered annotation, and phased equipment procurement directly addresses enrollment speed, scoring reliability, and cost control. Parallel safety and privacy frameworks ensure ethical compliance without stalling operations. Finally, a staged event‑triage algorithm balances manual labor reduction with fidelity to human adjudication. Together, these levers align the unit’s operational, scientific, and regulatory objectives, enabling the 3‑year plan to meet its enrollment, data‑quality, and publication milestones.

levers.name: Recruitment Channel Optimization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever accelerates participant enrollment, but may increase screening workload and risk of enrolling ineligible subjects. It also raises the cost per participant if additional triage resources are required.
options: Implement a hybrid referral pipeline that relies on hospital EMR alerts, patient portal outreach, and community outreach events.
Implement a referral pipeline that uses targeted physician outreach, patient self-referral portals, and regional sleep clinic collaborations.
Implement a referral pipeline that incorporates automated triage algorithms, nurse-led screening calls, and partnership with national sleep disorder registries.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing rapid enrollment against screening accuracy; options overlook the need for real-time eligibility verification during triage, risking over-enrollment of ineligible participants.

levers.name: Annotation Workflow Efficiency
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reduces manual scoring time, but may compromise inter‑rater reliability if shortcuts are adopted. It also increases reliance on technician training and may extend shift durations.
options: Implement a tiered annotation system that assigns preliminary scoring to trained technicians, followed by double-checks by senior reviewers.
Implement a collaborative annotation platform that synchronizes real-time scoring across multiple reviewers, enabling instant consensus and error flagging.
Implement a semi-automated annotation workflow that uses rule-based event detection to pre-label segments, then requires human confirmation before final scoring.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: speeding annotation while maintaining quality; options ignore the impact of technician fatigue on scoring consistency over long shifts.

levers.name: Equipment Acquisition Strategy
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever influences data fidelity and budget; selecting high-end sensors may improve event capture but increase maintenance complexity and long-term costs.
options: Implement a phased procurement plan that prioritizes essential dry-electrode headbands and mattress sensors, adding full PSG equipment only after pilot validation.
Implement a modular equipment architecture that allows interchangeable sensor modules, enabling rapid upgrades and parallel testing of new acquisition technologies.
Implement a cost-sharing partnership with a medical device manufacturer to secure discounted equipment in exchange for anonymized data access for future research.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing data quality with budget constraints; options overlook the long-term maintenance costs and potential obsolescence of high-end PSG systems.

levers.name: Participant Safety and Ethics Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever enhances participant protection, but may increase administrative burden and delay enrollment; strict safety protocols could limit flexibility in event response.
options: Implement a continuous safety monitoring framework that integrates real-time vitals, automated alarm thresholds, and rapid-response staffing protocols.
Implement a participant education program that delivers pre-admission training videos, in-room safety checklists, and post-discharge follow-up calls.
Implement a risk stratification algorithm that flags high-risk participants for additional overnight monitoring and tailored safety measures.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: ensuring safety without stifling recruitment; options fail to address how safety measures might deter participants with mild symptoms.

levers.name: Data Sharing and Privacy Governance
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever increases transparency and scientific impact, but may expose sensitive data and require robust governance; public sharing is limited to de-identified data.
options: Implement a tiered data access model that grants researchers controlled, de-identified datasets while preserving participant anonymity through strict encryption.
Implement a data use agreement framework that requires external researchers to sign confidentiality clauses and submit detailed data handling plans.
Implement a community advisory board that reviews data sharing requests and ensures alignment with local ethical standards and participant expectations.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: balancing openness with privacy; options ignore the potential for re-identification through cross-referencing ancillary data.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reduces manual review load, but algorithm accuracy must be validated against human scorers; risk of false positives may increase workload.
options: Implement a supervised learning pipeline that trains classifiers on manually scored events, then applies threshold tuning to balance sensitivity and specificity.
Implement a hybrid rule-based system that flags events based on physiological signatures, followed by human confirmation to refine model parameters.
Implement an iterative feedback loop that incorporates scorer adjudication outcomes into continuous model retraining, ensuring performance improves over time.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: reducing workload while maintaining accuracy; options overlook the need for ongoing human oversight to prevent drift in algorithm performance.

responses.strategic_rationale: The residential parasomnia unit must balance operational efficiency, participant experience, data integrity, regulatory compliance, and financial viability. Lever selection targets core tensions: maximizing suite utilization while protecting staff and equipment, retaining participants to meet event yield targets, integrating multi‑modal longitudinal data without overwhelming infrastructure, synchronizing clinical workflows to reduce response latency, ensuring GDPR and trial compliance to avoid legal setbacks, and securing diversified funding to sustain expansion. These levers collectively address speed, cost, scope, and quality trade‑offs inherent in early‑stage clinical research, guiding decision‑makers toward a resilient, scalable model.

levers.name: Sleep Suite Utilization Optimization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever increases occupancy of suites, reducing idle nights and maximizing data yield, but may raise staff workload and equipment wear. Higher utilization can also compress participant comfort if suites are overbooked, potentially affecting adherence.
options: Deploy a predictive scheduling system that allocates participants to suites based on anticipated event frequency, maximizing nightly data yield while preserving buffer capacity.
Introduce a rotating maintenance protocol that performs overnight cleaning and calibration on each suite, extending equipment lifespan without interrupting participant stays.
Offer participants a choice between shared and private suites with tiered compensation, encouraging efficient space use while respecting individual comfort preferences.
levers.review_lever: Balancing suite occupancy against staff fatigue and equipment wear; the options overlook the need for real‑time workload monitoring to prevent overtime.

levers.name: Participant Retention and Engagement
levers.consequences: Enhancing retention improves adherence and event capture, but raises per‑participant costs and demands additional support staff. Over‑engagement may also pressure participants, impacting data quality.
options: Deploy a personalized engagement protocol that schedules regular check‑ins and delivers tailored sleep‑hygiene resources, fostering participant commitment throughout the stay.
Introduce a gamified progress dashboard that displays nightly sleep metrics and offers small incentives for consistent participation, motivating sustained engagement.
Provide flexible meal and activity options that align with participants’ cultural preferences, reducing perceived burden and encouraging longer stays.
levers.review_lever: Enhancing retention may raise per‑participant costs and staff demands; the options ignore the need for scalable support infrastructure.

levers.name: Longitudinal Data Integration
levers.consequences: Integrating multi‑modal streams across nights enriches phenotyping, yet increases computational load and storage needs. Complex harmonization may also delay analysis.
options: Deploy a modular data ingestion framework that automatically tags and aligns multi‑modal sensor streams in real time, facilitating seamless longitudinal aggregation.
Implement a cloud‑based analytics layer that aggregates nightly datasets, enabling cross‑night comparisons without imposing local storage bottlenecks.
Introduce a standardized metadata schema that captures contextual variables such as medication changes and sleep environment, supporting longitudinal trend analysis.
levers.review_lever: Integrating longitudinal data enhances phenotyping but may overwhelm storage and processing; the options overlook the need for incremental data compression strategies.

levers.name: Clinical Workflow Synchronization
levers.consequences: Aligning technician shifts with event patterns improves response times, but reduces flexibility for unexpected events and adds coordination overhead.
options: Deploy a real‑time event alert system that notifies technicians via wearable devices, ensuring immediate response to flagged events.
Implement a shift‑rotation schedule that aligns technician coverage with peak event windows identified from historical data, optimizing response efficiency.
Introduce a cross‑disciplinary briefing protocol that synchronizes sleep physicians, technicians, and data engineers before each night, reducing miscommunication.
levers.review_lever: Synchronizing workflows boosts response speed but may constrain flexibility for emergent events; the options miss contingency planning for technician absences.

levers.name: Regulatory Compliance Alignment
levers.consequences: Ensuring GDPR and trial compliance reduces legal risk, but can slow study initiation and increase administrative burden. It may also limit data sharing scope.
options: Create a dedicated compliance officer position that monitors evolving GDPR and clinical trial guidelines, ensuring timely protocol adjustments.
Implement a centralized audit trail platform that logs every data access and consent update, enabling rapid regulatory inspections.
Introduce a participant education module that explains data usage and privacy rights, fostering informed consent and reducing future disputes.
levers.review_lever: Aligning with regulations protects the study but may delay recruitment; the options overlook the need for a phased consent strategy.

levers.name: Funding Sustainability Strategy
levers.consequences: Securing diverse funding supports expansion and advanced analytics, yet requires additional reporting and stakeholder engagement. It may shift priorities toward funding metrics.
options: Build a tiered grant portfolio that targets national, European, and industry funding streams, diversifying revenue sources.
Implement a transparent financial dashboard that tracks budget utilization against milestones, offering stakeholders real‑time insight into resource allocation.
Introduce a stakeholder advisory panel that convenes quarterly to review progress and align funding priorities with scientific objectives.
levers.review_lever: Securing diverse funding supports growth but may impose reporting burdens; the options ignore the need for automated compliance reporting.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project must balance high‑fidelity data capture with operational feasibility, participant safety, and budget constraints. By targeting environmental calibration, real‑time escalation, data integration, ethical video oversight, longitudinal follow‑up, and PSG resource allocation, we address core tensions: speed of data acquisition versus quality, cost of equipment and staffing versus scientific rigor, scope of multimodal data versus privacy, and participant burden versus depth of phenotyping. These levers collectively enable a scalable, ethically sound, and scientifically robust residential unit that can meet enrollment, event capture, and publication milestones while remaining within the €3.8 M budget and regulatory framework.

levers.name: Participant Sleep Environment Calibration
levers.consequences: Adjusting room temperature, lighting, and acoustic conditions to mirror natural circadian cues increases the likelihood of capturing authentic parasomnia events. This may raise equipment and maintenance costs and require staff training to manage dynamic settings.
options: Deploy a dynamic environmental control system that adjusts room temperature and lighting to mirror natural circadian cues.
Implement a standardized pre‑sleep routine protocol that participants follow each night to reduce variability in baseline arousal.
Introduce a nightly acoustic masking protocol that uses low‑frequency soundscapes to dampen external noise.
levers.review_lever: Core tension between environmental realism and operational complexity; options overlook the need to validate that adjustments do not interfere with sensor signal integrity.

levers.name: Real‑Time Event Escalation Protocol
levers.consequences: A structured escalation workflow for flagged events enables rapid deployment of full PSG, improving data resolution for rare episodes. It increases technician workload and may delay monitoring of other nights.
options: Establish a tiered alert system that triggers full PSG deployment within 30 minutes of technician flagging a suspected event.
Create a shared digital log that allows technicians to annotate and prioritize events in real time, enabling coordinated escalation.
Introduce a rotating on‑call specialist who reviews flagged events during the night and decides on PSG escalation.
levers.review_lever: Tension between rapid data capture and staffing constraints; options miss the impact of on‑call specialist fatigue on decision quality.

levers.name: Cross‑Disciplinary Data Integration
levers.consequences: Consolidating physiological, behavioral, and environmental streams into a unified platform enriches phenotyping but adds computational and governance complexity.
options: Build a centralized data warehouse that consolidates EEG, video, audio, and sensor data for joint analysis.
Deploy a modular analytics pipeline that processes each data modality separately before merging results for cross‑validation.
Implement a real‑time fusion engine that overlays multimodal signals to flag potential events automatically.
levers.review_lever: Core tension between data richness and privacy safeguards; options ignore the need for robust de‑identification before cross‑modality merging.

levers.name: Ethical Oversight of Video Recording
levers.consequences: Strengthening safeguards around video capture improves participant trust and recruitment willingness, but introduces stricter consent procedures and potential enrollment delays.
options: Introduce a participant‑controlled video pause button that can be activated during any night.
Implement a third‑party video audit system that reviews footage for privacy compliance before storage.
Offer participants the option to have video recordings deleted after a set period, with data retained only in anonymized form.
levers.review_lever: Tension between data utility and privacy; options fail to address how deletion requests affect longitudinal data continuity.

levers.name: Longitudinal Participant Follow‑Up Strategy
levers.consequences: Systematic post‑discharge monitoring extends the data set and deepens phenotyping, yet increases participant burden and risks attrition.
options: Schedule monthly home‑based actigraphy sessions for participants after discharge to monitor ongoing episodes.
Provide participants with a wearable sleep tracker that syncs data to the central repository for continuous monitoring.
Conduct quarterly telehealth check‑ins that include structured sleep diaries and symptom questionnaires.
levers.review_lever: Tension between longitudinal depth and participant compliance; options overlook the challenge of ensuring data quality from home‑based devices.

levers.name: Resource Allocation for Enhanced PSG Nights
levers.consequences: Optimizing budget and staffing for PSG nights improves event resolution but may create bottlenecks in technician availability and increase costs.
options: Allocate a dedicated PSG technician for each enhanced night, ensuring full coverage and rapid setup.
Use a shared technician pool that rotates between enhanced and standard nights, balancing workload.
Implement a remote PSG setup protocol that allows technicians to configure equipment from a control room, reducing on‑site presence.
levers.review_lever: Core tension between data fidelity and cost efficiency; options miss the impact of remote setup on signal quality and participant comfort.

options: Implement a hybrid referral pipeline that relies on hospital EMR alerts, patient portal outreach, and community outreach events.
Implement a referral pipeline that uses targeted physician outreach, patient self-referral portals, and regional sleep clinic collaborations.
Implement a referral pipeline that incorporates automated triage algorithms, nurse-led screening calls, and partnership with national sleep disorder registries.
levers.review: Core tension: balancing rapid enrollment against screening accuracy; options overlook the need for real-time eligibility verification during triage, risking over-enrollment of ineligible participants.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
